original_abstract: Nearly a century after its discovery, Fischer–Tropsch synthesis (FTS) remains a focal point of scientific debate and industrial importance. This review examines the recent shift toward a molecular-level understanding of the process, driven by the integration of surface science, density functional theory (DFT), and microkinetic modeling. For cobalt-based catalysts, significant progress has been made in characterizing the structure of nanoparticles and the formation of stable CO overlayers. Modern simulations and experimental data are converging on a consensus regarding active sites—specifically the role of terraced surfaces versus step-edges—and the debate between carbide and CO-insertion mechanisms. In contrast, iron-based catalysts present a more complex challenge due to the dynamic evolution of multiple oxide and carbide phases under reaction conditions. While current research has begun to clarify the stability of these phases and the influence of chemical promoters, a definitive molecular "movie" of the active site remains incomplete. Finally, the paper highlights the emerging relevance of CO2-FTS as a sustainable pathway for producing long-chain hydrocarbons, discussing how existing molecular insights into CO hydrogenation can be adapted to advance carbon capture and utilization technologies.
humanized_abstract: A century after its discovery Fischer–Tropsch synthesis (FTS) remains one of the most studied processes in catalysis both scientifically and industrially. During the last decade the FTS field has experienced a paradigm shift in terms of the focus from macroscopic studies to molecular level descriptions of the process using complementary surface science, density functional theory (DFT) and microkinetic modelling approaches. This has allowed for deeper understanding of the cobalt based FTS catalysts such as describing the structure of the catalytically active nanoparticle and the characteristics of the stable CO overlayers formed on them. It has also allowed for consensus on the catalytic sites on such catalysts such as the role of terraces versus edges and carbide versus CO insertion mechanisms. In contrast iron-based catalysts remain to be fully understood at the molecular level due to their complexity which stems from the dynamic operation of a multitude of oxide and carbide species under FTS conditions. Although considerable progress has been reported in understanding the stability of the iron species present under FTS and the effect of promoters on them, a detailed "movie" of the molecular catalytic site is not yet at the hands. This is also due to the emerging interest in the area of CO2-FTS and the use of long chain hydrocarbons as feedstocks. Understanding the molecular level concepts derived from studying the process of CO hydrogenation can shed the light towards further advancement of the carbon utilisation technology and the CCU process in general.
